name: Guadix Badlands Villages Loop
name_original: Los Badlands de Guadix — pueblos de Beas y Cogollos de Guadix
description: Explore the unique Badlands of Guadix on this 29.2-mile (47.0 km) road cycling route, passing through Beas and Cogollos de Guadix.
region_intro: Road cycling around Guadix offers diverse terrain characterized by arid badlands, unique geological formations, and the northern foothills of the Sierra Nevada mountains. The region features a mix of open plains, eroded clay and sandstone structures, and undulating roads. Cyclists can experience varied elevation changes, from routes through the Hoya de Guadix plain to more challenging climbs towards the mountain ranges. Best road cycling routes around Guadix The most popular road cycling route is Badlands of the Hoya de Guadix and Río Fardes Loop, a 66.3 miles (106.8 km) difficult trail that takes about 4 hours 50 minutes to complete. This route explores the distinctive badlands landscape and follows the Río Fardes. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Guadix Badlands Villages Loop, a moderate 29.2 miles (47.0 km) path. This route offers views of the unique badlands and connects several local villages. Local road cyclists also love the La Peza Fountain – Los Blancares Pass loop from Guadix, a 35.9 miles (57.8 km) trail leading through varied terrain with significant elevation gain, often completed in about 2 hours 56 minutes. Road cycling around Guadix is defined by arid badlands, the foothills of the Sierra Nevada, and unique cave house settlements. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate loops to challenging mountain climbs. The routes in Guadix are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.0 stars. More than 400 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Guadix's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Guadix?
A: There are over 30 road cycling routes around Guadix listed on komoot, offering a diverse range of experiences for cyclists. These routes vary in difficulty, from moderate loops through the badlands to challenging mountain climbs towards the Sierra Nevada foothills.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Guadix?
A: Road cycling around Guadix features a unique and varied terrain. You'll encounter arid badlands with eroded clay and sandstone structures, especially prominent in the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4101272" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gorafe Desert&lt;/a&gt;. Routes also traverse the open Hoya de Guadix plain and include undulating roads leading to the northern foothills of the Sierra Nevada mountains, offering significant elevation changes.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Guadix?
A: The best time for road cycling in Guadix is typically during the spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November). During these seasons, temperatures are milder and more comfortable for cycling, allowing you to fully enjoy the region's unique landscapes without the intense heat of summer or the colder conditions of winter, especially at higher elevations.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Guadix?
A: Yes, Guadix offers routes suitable for various fitness levels. While many routes involve undulating terrain, there are easier options available. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e983769471/guadix-badlands-villages-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guadix Badlands Villages Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 47 km path that provides a good introduction to the region's unique badlands scenery without extreme difficulty.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes in Guadix for experienced cyclists?
A: Absolutely. Guadix is an excellent destination for experienced cyclists seeking a challenge. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e984053825/badlands-of-the-hoya-de-guadix-and-rio-fardes-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Badlands of the Hoya de Guadix and Río Fardes Loop&lt;/a&gt;, at over 100 km with significant elevation gain, offer demanding rides. The region's proximity to the Sierra Nevada also means routes with substantial ascents, such as those leading towards Puerto de la Ragua, are available for those looking for mountain climbing experiences.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see while cycling in Guadix?
A: You'll encounter stunning natural features while cycling. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4101272" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gorafe Desert&lt;/a&gt; and its impressive red-hued rock formations, known as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5459236" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Coloraos Badlands&lt;/a&gt;, are a highlight. You can also experience the Marchal Gullies Natural Monument and enjoy panoramic views from viewpoints like the Mirador del Fin del Mundo. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4101927" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Granada Geopark&lt;/a&gt; is another significant natural area to explore.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural attractions accessible by bike in Guadix?
A: Yes, many routes allow you to explore the rich history and culture of Guadix. The famous Barrio de las Cuevas (Cave Quarter), with its unique cave houses, is a must-see. You can also cycle past the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4852722" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guadix Cathedral&lt;/a&gt; and the Alcazaba de Guadix. A route like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31472113" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cave Quarter, Guadix – Castillo de La Calahorra loop from Guadix&lt;/a&gt; combines natural beauty with historical sights.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes in Guadix?
A: Many of the road cycling routes in Guadix are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. This includes popular options like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31560790" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Peza Fountain – Los Blancares Pass loop from Guadix&lt;/a&gt;, which offers varied terrain and significant elevation gain, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31466420" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Acequia del Toril – Cave Quarter, Guadix loop from Guadix&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Guadix?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.0 stars. Cyclists often praise the unique and dramatic landscapes, particularly the badlands and the views of the Sierra Nevada. The diverse network of routes catering to different abilities and the opportunity to explore charming villages and historical sites are also frequently highlighted.

Q: Are there any dedicated cycling paths or Vías Verdes in the Guadix area?
A: Yes, the region features the Vía Verde del FC. Guadix-Almendricos, a dedicated cycling path spanning over 20 km. This 'greenway' offers a more relaxed cycling experience away from traffic, following a former railway line and allowing you to enjoy the scenery at a leisurely pace.

Q: Can I find places to eat or stay along the road cycling routes in Guadix?
A: Guadix and the surrounding villages offer various options for refreshments and accommodation. Many routes pass through or near towns where you can find cafes, restaurants, and places to stay. It's advisable to plan your route and check for services in advance, especially when venturing into more remote areas of the badlands or mountains.
tips: Beas de Guadix is a town that is right next to Cerro de la Noria and the Mirador del Fin del Mundo. These pair of small hills that surround the mentioned nucleus, are characterized by land without vegetation that form characteristic "Beas".
Cogollos de Guadix is an Andalusian municipality located in the province of Granada. It is an urban center of Muslim origin, nestled between the Lugros River and the Barranc del Bernal.
The most beautiful view of Guadix with its many cave houses. You should definitely take a look here if you are in Guadix, as it is the most beautiful place in the town.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This moderate road cycling route, covering approximately 47.0 km with an elevation gain of 660 meters, typically takes around 2 hours and 27 minutes to complete. This duration is for active cycling time and does not include stops for photos or refreshments.

faqs.question: What is the starting point for this cycling route, and is parking available?
faqs.answer: The route passes through villages like Beas de Guadix and Cogollos de Guadix. Parking is generally available in or near these villages, which serve as convenient starting points for the loop. Specific parking areas may vary, so it's advisable to check local signage upon arrival.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginner road cyclists?
faqs.answer: Rated as a 'moderate road ride,' this route requires good fitness due to its consistent elevation gain. While the surfaces are mostly well-paved, the hilly terrain ensures a consistent workout. Beginners with a good level of fitness and some experience with road cycling hills may find it manageable, but it's not an easy, flat ride.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain and road surface can I expect on this route?
faqs.answer: The route is primarily on mostly well-paved surfaces, making it easy to ride for road bikes. However, the broader Badlands area is known for varied terrain. While this specific route is designed for road cycling, if you plan any detours, be aware that some unpaved sections could range from compacted dirt to rougher gravel. For optimal comfort, especially if exploring, a road bike with slightly wider tires or a gravel bike could be advantageous.

faqs.question: What unique natural features and scenery will I encounter?
faqs.answer: You'll cycle through an otherworldly landscape of ochre and reddish tones, often compared to a lunar landscape, shaped by millions of years of erosion. The striking colors of the earth, contrasted with sparse vegetation, create a visually dramatic environment. Views of the snow-capped Sierra Nevada mountains frequently serve as a breathtaking backdrop. The region is also notable for its cave dwellings, with many people still residing in these unique homes carved into the hillsides.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle the Badlands of Guadix?
faqs.answer: The Badlands of Guadix are best explored during spring and autumn when temperatures are milder. Summers can be very hot, making cycling challenging, while winters can bring colder temperatures, especially at higher elevations, though the region generally experiences many sunny days.

faqs.question: Are there any notable landmarks or viewpoints along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route offers several points of interest. You'll pass through the charming villages of Beas de Guadix and Cogollos de Guadix, known for their unique cave houses. A significant highlight is the Padre Poveda Viewpoint of Guadix, which offers panoramic vistas of the dramatic Badlands landscape and the distant Sierra Nevada.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other official cycling or hiking trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route intersect with several other official paths. Notably, it covers portions of the Camino Mozárabe. Almería-Granada, Transnevada (Stage 1 / 8), and Transnevada (Stage 2 / 8), among others. This offers opportunities to extend your ride or explore different segments of the wider trail network.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While dogs are generally welcome in many outdoor areas, specific regulations for cycling routes can vary. As this is primarily a road cycling route, dogs would need to be safely secured in a trailer or basket if accompanying you. Always ensure your dog's safety and comfort, and respect any local signage regarding pets.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for cycling in the Geoparque de Granada?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route within the Geoparque de Granada. The Geoparque is an open landscape, and access to public roads and trails is generally free. However, always respect private property and follow local regulations.

faqs.question: What should I pack or consider bringing for this ride?
faqs.answer: Given the moderate difficulty and potential for varied weather, it's advisable to bring sufficient water, snacks, sun protection (sunscreen, hat, sunglasses), and appropriate cycling attire. A basic repair kit for your bike is also recommended. If you plan to explore off-paved sections, consider a bike with slightly wider tires.

faqs.question: Are there places to stop for refreshments or food along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, as the route passes through villages like Beas de Guadix and Cogollos de Guadix, you will find opportunities to stop for refreshments. These traditional villages often have local cafes, bars, or small shops where you can grab a drink or a bite to eat.
